CSCE 314 Syllabus

Section 501 (51967)

Programming Languages

Spring 2026 - College Station

Course Information

Meeting Times: (Sec 500)  Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: TR

Start Time: 09:35AM

End Time: 10:50AM

Start Date: 01/12/2026

End Date: 05/05/2026

Meeting Location:  ZACH 310

Meeting Times: (Sec 501) Meeting Type: LEC

Meeting Days: MWF

Start Time: 10:20AM

End Time: 11:10AM

Meeting Location:  ZACH 310

Start Date:   08/25/2025

End Date:  12/16/2025

Credit Hours:  3

Catalog Description

Programming Languages. (3-0). Credit 3. Exploration of the design space of

programming languages via an in-depth study of two programming languages, one

functional and one object-oriented; focuses on idiomatic uses of each language and

on features characteristic for each language. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in

CSCE 221, or concurrent enrollment.

Additional Course Details

This course explores the design space of programming languages via an in-depth

study of the most common features and constructs in programming languages,

using two programming languages to illustrate them: Haskell (Functional) and Java

(object-oriented).

The course focuses on idiomatic uses of functional and object-oriented languages

and on feature characteristics of those categories of languages. This course goes

into details of how programs written in high-level programming languages are

executed.

This includes topics such as static and dynamic environments, internal program

representations, type checking, and interpreting. Students will learn about the

implementation approaches of common abstraction mechanisms and modern

programming language constructs. The course explores the use of the functional

programming approach to design and implement programs, as well as study the

main features of object-oriented languages.

Although the course uses and studies the above specific programming languages,

learning these languages should be considered as a side benefit of the course, not

the main goal.

programming language constructs. The course explores the use of the functional

programming approach to design and implement programs, as well as study the

main features of object-oriented languages.

Although the course uses and studies the above specific programming languages,

learning these languages should be considered as a side benefit of the course, not

the main goal.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 221, or concurrent

enrollment.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, students will be able to:

Use modern programming languages effectively

Develop the necessary skills to learn and adopt new programming languages

Evaluate the power of programming languages and their constructs

Apply language constructs, common abstraction mechanisms, and efficiency

considerations

Analyze the meaning and efficiency of functional and object-oriented

programs

Master many specific language concepts and use them effectively to build

correct and elegant software

Although the course uses and studies specific programming languages, learning

these languages should be considered as a side benefit of the course, not the main

goal.

Special Course Designation

None

Textbook and/or Resource Materials

This material Is: Required

No Textbook Needed

Notes:

Required   textbooks   and   reading   materials   will   be   provided

electronically   through   Perusall   and   linked   on   Canvas .

To   receive   credit ,  each   and   all   individual   Perusall   reading

assignments   must   be   clicked   on   in   Canvas .  The   grades   will   not

transfer   otherwise .

Grading Policy

Regrading

Regrade requests may be made up to one week after grades are published. You

must include justification for the change.

We are here to help, but it is the student’s responsibility to track progress and seek

help before falling behind.

Class Participation

Participation is expected. In-class assignments that demonstrate understanding of

the presented topics are counted as part of your grade.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late work is generally not accepted without an excused absence.

It is your responsibility to let us know if you missed something and what date you

the presented topics are counted as part of your grade.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Course Specific Late Work Policy

Late work is generally not accepted without an excused absence.

It is your responsibility to let us know if you missed something and what date you

can have it completed by.

Course Schedule

Overview

Functional programming. In the weeks before spring break, we will work on

Functional programming using Haskell. There will be weekly assignments.

Midterm exam, Thursday, March 5th, during class.

Object-oriented programming. In the weeks after spring break, we will work

on OOP using Java. There will be weekly assignments.

A final project will be due the last week of the semester.

The final exam is on Friday, May 1, 1 PM to 3 PM for section 500, and

Thursday, April 30, 12:30 PM to 2:30 PMfor section 501

AI Statement

The use of AI to gain a greater understanding of a topic is a great use of its

capabilities. This is allowed.

The use of AI to do your assignments is a poor use of its capabilities, as it does not

increase your capabilities. This is highly discouraged. If done, you must cite the AI

tool you used and give the prompts and responses given/received.

There is no access to any form of AI during exams, and historically, those who used

AI to complete assignments did poorly on exams.
